entry.title: New Learning Journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance
entry.content:
Why This Learning Journey Matters

Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping how organizations operate, from automating routine processes to supporting smarter decision-making. Finance professionals who understand how to apply AI in real business scenarios are becoming essential change agents.

This intermediate-level learning journey is built for learners who already understand core finance processes and are ready to take the next step: applying SAP Business AI capabilities directly within SAP Finance scenarios.

What You’ll Learn

This learning journey provides a guided, hands-on exploration of SAP’s latest AI innovations for finance, including:

  • SAP Joule in Finance
    See how SAP’s AI-powered digital assistant supports day-to-day finance tasks and insights.
  • SAP Joule Agents
    Learn how specialized AI agents can assist with more complex and cross-functional finance activities.
  • SAP Joule for Consultants
    Discover AI-driven tools that help consultants analyze, explain, and design finance solutions more effectively.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance
    Explore how intelligent, harmonized data enables advanced reporting, planning, and decision support.

Throughout the journey, learners follow practical examples and concrete finance use cases, bridging the gap between theory and real-world application.

Who Should Take This Learning Journey?

This learning journey is ideal for Finance consultants and solution architects, as well as Finance business experts interested in AI-driven innovation. Basic knowledge of SAP financial business processes is recommended to get the most value from the content. If you’re new to AI in finance, SAP recommends starting with a foundational journey such as Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance, before continuing with this more advanced, application-focused journey. 

Get Started Today

👉Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Upskill, experiment, and position yourself at the forefront of AI-powered finance transformation.

entry.title: How to Add Custom Field in Purchase Requisition Item in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Step-by-Step)
entry.content:
Business Requirement

In one of my clients, there is a requirement where we need to capture Department information at the Purchase Requisition (PR) Item level for:

  • Internal tracking
  • Reporting & analytics

However, this field is not available as standard in SAP Public Cloud.

Solution

There is a tile called Custom Fields, where we can create a custom field, and then we can assign it to PR. The detailed steps are below:

Step 1: Create Custom Field

> Navigate to the Custom Fields App as shown below:

Create a new field as shown below :

  • Label: DEPARTMENT

  • Identifier: YY1_DEPARTMENT

  • Business Context:
    Procurement: Purchase Requisition Item (MM_PURREQN_ITEM)

  • Field Type: Code List

Here, we are choosing codel because it fulfils my requirement where we can assigned muliplte pre defined value, like how we did in the ECC & S/4 Hana on-prem when creating a domain:

Step 2: Maintain Code List Values

Define department values:

Code Description

ACCAccounts
AUDAudit
ENGEngineering
ITIT
MKT1Marketing-I
MKT2Marketing-II
SSStock & Store

This allows dropdown/value help in UI

Step 3: Maintain UI Texts

Configure labels properly:

  • Label: DEPARTMENT (Desc.)

  • Short Label: Dept.

  • Medium Label: Dept.D

This ensures proper display across UI screens.

Step 4: Enable Field Usage

Go to the User Interfaces tab and enable usage in:

CDS Views

  • C_EXTPURCHASEREQUISITIONITEM

  • I_PURCHASEREQUISITION_API01

  • I_PURCHASEREQUISITIONITEMAPI01

SAP GUI

  • PR_ITEM_GUI (Purchase Requisition Item GUI)

This is a very important step (most people miss this)

Step 5: Publish the Field

Click Publish

Once published:

  • Field is added to the database structure

  • Available for UI & API consumption

Step 6: Test in Purchase Requisition

Open Purchase Requisition screen.

New Tab Appears:

Department

Inside tab:

  • Field: Department

  • Value Help enabled

Behaviour in System

  • User selects department from the dropdown

  • Values are controlled via Code List

  • Field is stored at the PR Item level

Key Technical Points

  • Field is automatically extended in:

    • CDS Views

    • OData Services

    • UI annotations

  • No need for manual ABAP coding

  • Fully compliant with the SAP Public Cloud extensibility model

Advantages

✔️ No modification required
✔️ Works in both SAP GUI & Fiori
✔️ Reusable across APIs & reports
✔️ Easy maintenance via Code List

Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Not enabling CDS views
❌ Forgetting SAP GUI enablement
❌ Not publishing after changes
❌ Incorrect business context selection

Conclusion

Custom Fields in SAP Public Cloud provide a powerful, upgrade-safe way to enhance standard applications.

With minimal effort, we successfully:
✔️Added Department field
✔️Enabled dropdown values
✔️Integrated with UI & backend

This approach can be reused for multiple business requirements across procurement and finance.

Personal Experience:

If this requirement for ECC or S/4 Hana on-prem, we could have done it in the old traditional way. But this public cloud experience is on another level, the satisfaction I got while doing the research about it can be compared to how we used to search user exits and customer exits at first time when entering into SAP. Even tho SAP has restricted most things for public cloud, it also provides us an easy solution, as we can see it here. There is one more app called Custom Logic, where we can write code for the validation, which we will discuss later.

Please feel free to connect if you have any queries.

Happy Learning 😀

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026: Getting past the AI skills gap with help from your ASUG peers
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is about connection. One of SAP’s goals for the event is to connect its customers with the right solution to solve their business needs. The keynotes, customer demos, and product showcases focus on this. Another key objective is bringing people together - customers, partners, and SAP experts - to have meaningful conversations about problems enterprises are facing and how to address them. The sessions in ASUG’s Community Hub provide a structured framework to tackle complex topics such as how to adopt AI in your organization.

How are you feeling about AI in your workplace? Answer in the chat below.

Why we care about AI and adoption

Nicole Smythe and I are facilitating an ASUG Community Conversation on how to overcome barriers to AI implementation in your organization (ERP1864). Our goal is to help participants create a 90-day plan to turn your thoughts into action and move forward with AI in your organizations.

Nicole leads product marketing for AI in SAP Cloud ERP Private, so she is very focused on ensuring that customers obtain the maximum value from their investments in SAP. My team, Cloud ERP UX Foundation, creates embedded AI capabilities for SAP Cloud ERP, so we want people to use what we build. I’m also active in ASUG (co-lead for SAP Cloud ERP community and Northern California chapter) and the SAP Community. We’re both excited to meet with customers to find out what is holding them back from going all-in on AI.

SAP has many options for AI: embedded and Joule, base and premium

SAP has many options for AI, but which is best for you depends on your organizational readiness. Organizational readiness is not based on what top executives say about using AI, it’s about how individual people use AI to make their jobs more efficient. And usage only happens if people have the knowledge about the AI tools and technologies and how to use them effectively in their specific roles.

SAP has a range of AI solutions to address different business challenges.

In our various interactions with customers, we are finding a range of feelings from optimism to fear about using AI in their jobs. Sometimes we see a mix of feelings in the same person. Yes, AI can be very confusing. One thing that most people agree on is that they are not confident their organizations have the skills to succeed with AI.

Bridging the AI skills gap starts with identifying the specific challenges

That is the purpose of our ASUG workshop. We want to address the skills gap and get people talking about what barriers they need to overcome to implement AI in their organizations. We’ll split into groups and people can discuss what skills they feel they are missing and brainstorm ideas on how to overcome one or two challenges.

If you are going to Sapphire and are thinking about rolling out AI in your organization, please join us to learn some ideas about how to accelerate your progress. If you have already figured this out, please join us to share your knowledge with your peers.

To help us prepare for what’s important to you, please comment below.

  • How are you feeling about AI in your workplace?
  • Where do you think AI will have the biggest impact?
  • How are you measuring the impact of AI in your organization?
  • What skills do you think are most important for successful AI usage?
  • What skills are missing from your organization that keeps you from mass adoption?

The SAP Community is another safe space to have these conversations through a combination of learning, coaching, and engaging with peers. Please join us online at https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/business-ai  to talk about AI and how it can make your work life better.  Follow the AI group to learn more about what SAP is doing.

See you at Sapphire!

entry.title: Building Your Simple Business Roles with the IAM Apps
entry.content:
Introduction

In my blog Precise Control of Fiori App Access with the App Authorization Variants, I discussed newly available IAM Apps for the App Authorization Variant type (too wordy and hard to read! I call it IAM Apps or AAVs in this blog interchangeably if we confine the discussion in this context) to precisely control the Fiori app access from Release 2502.  This approach still performs authorization under the big umbrella of the Business Catalogs.

In this blog, I am going to discuss a new approach with an example: creating your simple business roles with the IAM Apps without using business catalogs. 

Background

I use a very simple case to illustrate the key point here.  Below figure shows a typical authorization case of one Business Role provides access to three Fiori Apps via one Business Catalog.

One Business Role Controls Three Apps through One Business Catalog

 For example, by exploring the Business Catalog SAP_CA_BC_IC_LND_AP_DSP_PC in a 2602 Release system, we find out it controls the access to three applications (see the red box below).  The reason is to form a semantically meaningful bundle of apps and app functions aligned to specific tasks or sub-processes. In a layman’s term, the Accounts Payable Configurator needs to access all these three applications to do the job. That is the intention we introduced and designed the concept of Business Catalogs for the SAP ERP Cloud.

The Business Catalog SAP_CA_BC_IC_LND_AP_DSP_PC Controls the Access to Three Apps

 

If we peel the onion with IAM App ID, we come out a new picture as shown below. Here we can observe the following:

  • App 1 has three IAM Apps IDs, A, B and C; each controls a specific transaction (remember, an app can contain one or more transactions as discussed in my previous blog)
  • App 2 has only one IAM Apps ID or associated with one transaction.
  • App 3 has no IAM Apps ID assigned, that makes App 3 always available if the Business Catalog is present.

One Business Role Controls Three Apps through One Business Catalog but with a Twist of IAM Apps

Still using the same Business Catalog SAP_CA_BC_IC_LND_AP_DSP_PC as an example, it can access three apps through each individual IAM App ID as highlighted in the above green box. It is simpler than my abstract illustration.

In the Authorization Model discussion of the SAP Help Portal, it mentions that IAM Apps ID can be assigned directly to a business role:

IAM apps can either be assigned directly to a business role or through a business catalog that bundles several IAM apps. The option to assign IAM apps directly is particularly useful when a business role requires only a few IAM apps from a specific business catalog. This approach helps you keep business roles lean without adding unnecessary business catalog content. However, if business catalogs represent tasks or sub-processes within a business process, it's best to continue assigning them to business roles. Then, activate only the IAM apps that are needed.

This means we can link the Business Role with Fiori Apps directly if there are IAM Apps as shown below.  In the case of App 3, we still need to use the Business Catalog to access it.

One Business Role Controls Three Apps through Four IAM Apps IDs and One Business Catalog

With this approach, we can effectively access App 1, run its embedded three transactions through three IAM Apps IDS, A, B and C.  The Business Catalog is no longer needed to access Apps 1-2, but the App 3.

While the IAM Apps approach provides an alternative way of creating a new Business Role, it is not advised to take this approach for all business roles as explained in above red text. It fits into simple use cases only.  Majority of SAP Business Role Templates are still Business Catalog based for good reasons.

Example

Let’s take a deep dive into one example, access the Fiori App Manage Business Users. From SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library, we found out the following:

  • The App ID is F1303
  • There are five Business Catalogs associated with it

Five Business Catalogs Control Access to the Fiori App Manage Business Users

 Since I intend to go around the business catalog route to create a business role, I am going to ignore these five business catalogs but take the advantage of App ID F1303 directly.  I create a business role Z_USER_MGMT from scratch. 

An Empty User Defined Business Role Z_USER_MGMT

In the IAM Apps Tab, I use F1303 App ID to do a search.  It returns three unique IAM App IDs corresponding to exactly five Business Catalogs listed in the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library. They are

  • F1303_03_TRAN                    for Displaying Business Users
  • F1303_22_TRAN                    for Assigning Business Users to Business Roles
  • F1303_TRAN                            for Maintaining Business Users

Three IAM App IDs associated with the App ID F1303

I select these three IAM App IDs to be part of my newly created user role Z_USER_MGMT. 

The User Role Z_USER_MGMT with three IAM App IDS

To ease the role consumption in the Fiori Launchpad, I assign the standard Launchpad Space SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR to the role.

The User Role Z_USER_MGMT with the standard Launchpad Space SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR

Last thing to do is assigning my test user testy as a business user to this role.  Save the role to update the role authorization.

A Complete User Role Z_USER_MGMT without Any Business Catalogs

In the Google Incognito window, I logged in as the user testy.  Since this user only has one role Z_USER_MGMT assigned, there is only one Page Identity and Access Management inherited from the Space SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR is shown.

My Home Page of the User testy

By clicking on the Administration tab next to My Home, or the Identity and Access Management Page, we can see the only Fiori App Maintain Business Users is available. All other spaces, pages and Fiori apps are unavailable due to limited authorizations.

After opening the app Maintain Business Users, the Edit button is grayed out. Why?

Cannot Edit a User in Maintain Business User app

I scratched my head for two days and finally was told by my colleague that there is one secret when creating a new business role: by default, the Write, Read, Value Help Access Category is marked as No Access. That prohibits users to make changes in the app.  I overlooked at this.

No Access Default Setting for the Access Category: Write, Read, Value Help

After selecting the right value of the Write, Read, Value Help Access Category, I can manage any users the same way as we have the SAP_BR_ADMINISTRATOR business role.  To further illustrate the effectiveness of these IAM Apps IDs, I removed F1303_22_TRAN IAM Apps ID. Now I cannot maintain the business roles as both the Add and Remove buttons are grayed out.

Add and Remove Buttons Are Grayed Out when IAM Apps ID F1303_22_TRAN Is Removed

Conclusion

This blog introduced a new approach of creating a simple Business Role by using the IAM Apps without Business Catalogs.  This approach suits the business scenario for a simple and straightforward business role. For a complicated scenario, especially a persona-based business role, using the Business Catalogs along with the IAM Apps serves better.

References

  • Blog: Precise Control of Fiori App Access with the App Authorization Variants
  • Blog: User Management in a Nutshell for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition
  • SAP Help: Work with IAM Apps (App Authorization Variants)
  • SAP Help: Authorization Model

entry.title: Rules for replicating WBS elements to MDI - sap s/4hana cloud public edition
entry.content:
You want to replicate WBS elements from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition-S4HC to Master Data Intergration However, you notice that while some WBS elements are replicated, some are not, some are with error message.

Here are the expected error message during replication:

A. Replication Aborted
1. 327(/s4ppm/crud) Element &1 not processed by MDI. Not an account assignment element

This message represents:
  1.a All milestones for Enterprise Project will not be replicated.
  1.b All project level for Professional Services Project will not be replicated.

2. 328(/s4ppm/crud)Element &1 not processed by MDI. All funcions are blocked
  2.a All workpackages for Professional Services, where the project is in the status of 'In Planning' will not be replicated. As its block functions are all set as 'Blocked'.
  2.b All WBS Element(Enterprise Project)/Workpackges(Professional Services) with processing status 'Locked(20)' and 'Closed(42)' will not be replicated. 

B. Replication posted with error messages, some fields are empty:
1. 325(/S4PPM/CRUD) System language &1 is a non-ISO language; element name not processed.
  The system language is not an ISO language, so element name will not be replicated
2. 077(WFD_WP1_MSG) Business partner does not exist for the user &1.
  The employee who is assigned as project manager has not been replicated to MDI workforce. So the project manager info will not be replicated.

entry.title: The Advanced Success Plan in Detail – Getting More Value from Your SAP Cloud Investment
entry.content:
In the second session of the SAP Success Plans webcast series in April 2026, Jesper Waaben, Chief Product Owner for the Advanced Success Plans in the ERP space, took a closer look at what the Advanced Success Plan actually delivers. While the first webcast covered the big picture across all three plan tiers, this session focused on the specifics — the services available, the new Success Pack format, and what has genuinely changed compared to the predecessor, Preferred Success.

Almost half of the attendees described themselves as being in the early stages of their cloud transformation, which makes the timing of this new offering particularly relevant. Find below a summary and here the slides and the english webcast recording.

What stays, what's new 

The Advanced Success Plan builds on familiar ground plus enters new territory.

What stays is the proactive partnership model, the technical and functional support, and the release guidance that customers already know from Preferred Success. Anyone who has worked with Preferred Success will recognize many of the elements.

What's new is where it gets interesting.

The most tangible change is what SAP calls Hands-on Activation. Under Preferred Success, SAP experts were not allowed to touch customer systems and everything was strictly "hands off." That has changed now. Experts can now activate new functionalities directly in the system and guide initial, lightweight configuration steps. This naturally needs to be coordinated with the project team and, where applicable, the implementation partner — but it removes a long-standing limitation that many customers found frustrating.

A second major addition is AI-powered Guidance, integrated into SAP Cloud ALM. It analyses system data, particularly for SAP S/4HANA Private and Public Cloud environments and derives recommendations for process improvements, feature adoption, and system landscape decisions.

Beyond that, the Advanced Plan now covers end-to-end scenarios across the full SAP Business Suite, comes with improved SLAs (50% faster initial response times and accelerated corrective actions compared to the Foundational Plan), and incorporates elements that previously required the more expensive Preferred Success Expanded Edition — meaning customers effectively get more for less.

The five pillars of the Advanced Success Plan from SAP

The Advanced Plan extends the Foundational Success Plan through five areas.

Innovation and Adoption Guidance is about SAP experts working together with the Customer Success Manager to analyse specific functionalities and processes within a given solution — including cross-functional scenarios. The output is a set of recommendations on what to improve and which new features to activate.

Activation and Optimization Services is where those recommendations get implemented. SAP consultants go into the system, activate functionalities, and guide first configuration steps. Waaben made a point of emphasising that the experts deployed here are experienced SAP consultants from project work, not a pure support organization.

AI-powered Quality and Process Guidance uses the data collected in Cloud ALM to generate insights and recommendations. The prerequisite is that Cloud ALM is actively used — without it, this particular value stream doesn't fully materialize.

Orchestration of the Success Plan is something Waaben described as frequently underestimated. Customer Success Managers and Success Plan Managers ensure the right services are deployed at the right time. The goal is to move away from a scattergun approach toward targeted service delivery. The orchestrators can pull in the right SAP experts for any topic across any solution.

Enhanced Support SLAs deliver 50% faster initial response times and accelerated corrective actions — a relevant factor for anyone running business-critical systems.

Graphic: Advanced & Foundational Success Plan

Service Maps and Success Packs - making complexity manageable

For each SAP solution — whether S/4HANA Public, S/4HANA Private, Supply Chain, Ariba, SuccessFactors, CX, or Business Data Cloud, there is a dedicated Service Map showing which services are available, ranging from focused functional services to transformative formats like the Cloud Mindset Workshop.

Across all solutions, SAP offers what it calls Success Expert Services, including Value Management (establishing a baseline for tracking value over the solution lifecycle), Service Planning (workshop-based mapping of the customer situation to the right services), Adoption Checkpoints (reviewing the solution for untapped potential), and Innovation Checkpoints (evaluating new releases and AI capabilities for the specific customer context).

As the number of available services grows, so does complexity. SAP addresses this through Success Packs, thematic bundles of related services. Examples include Implementation Essentials, Finance Foundation, and Finance Expansion for S/4HANA Public; Clean Core, Business AI CoE, and Finance Optimization for S/4HANA Private; and dedicated packs for Supply Chain, Ariba, SuccessFactors, CX, and BTP. Customers don't have to book every service within a pack. The packs serve as orientation and shift the conversation from individual service tickets to business processes and outcomes.

Business AI — structured support across every stage

AI was a recurring theme throughout the session. Waaben showed how the three plan tiers work together when it comes to AI adoption. The Foundational Plan provides initial guidance in a self-service format. The Advanced Plan adds expert advisory on governance, compliance, and Clean Core for AI, workshops to identify AI use cases, activation of Joule, Agents, and intelligent apps, as well as AI Business Readiness Workshops to prepare the organization. The Max Plan then takes identified use cases into prototyping and custom development.

This matters because the webcast audience data confirmed that AI and cloud migration are top-of-mind topics. When asked about their transformation focus, 38.5% of respondents cited cloud migration, 30.8% Clean Core, and 7.7% AI and Business Data Cloud — all areas where the Advanced Plan delivers targeted support.

This graphic also illustrate the benefit of Success Plans. The CSM orchestrates important tasks from discovery to activation to reap the full benefit of AI. Otherwise there may be a break after discovery of AI use-cases. With a Success Plan in Plan like the Advanced Plan you will have a coordinated structured plan and execution against that plan. 

Market validation of the Advanced Success Plan

Two independent studies by IDC and Forrester were conducted with a focus on Preferred Success but are applicable to the new model. The confirmed three key findings.

  • First, access to SAP expertise is business-critical given the scale of investment in SAP systems.
  • Second, business users expect faster returns from IT investments, which increases pressure on IT departments.
  • Third, AI is fundamentally changing the software landscape and investment horizons, and customers are actively seeking guidance.

According to both analyses, customers with Success Plans reach their project goals faster and with greater confidence. The market response to the new model has been strong. In the first two months after launch, more than 200 customers purchased the Advanced Success Plan.

Practical recommendations

For organizations considering the Advanced Success Plan, a few things are worth keeping in mind. Start using Cloud ALM early, as the AI-powered guidance depends on it, and early adoption means stronger analytical insights down the line.
Use Success Packs as your entry point rather than picking individual services from the catalogue; bundles like "Clean Core" or "Finance Expansion" provide better orientation. Engage your Customer Success Manager actively to identify the right services at the right time — it's not about volume but about fit.
Coordinate Hands-on Activation with your project team and implementation partner to avoid friction. Consider starting with an AI Business Readiness Workshop to prepare your organisation before diving into specific use cases. And set up Value Management early so the benefits of the Success Plan become measurable and easier to communicate internally.

Bottom line

The Advanced Success Plan is a meaningful evolution from Preferred Success. The shift from "hands off" to hands-on activation addresses a long-standing customer need, while Success Packs bring structure to an increasingly broad service portfolio. The integrated AI support across all plan tiers positions the Success Plans as a practical companion for organisations navigating their SAP cloud journey — whether the focus is on migration, optimisation, or preparing for AI.

Further information on the Success Plans is available in the overview blog: SAP Success Pläne – Der Guide zum neuen SAP Support und Service Modell 2026. An on-site event is also planned for 25 June 2026 in Walldorf. 

FAQ - Questions and Answers from the discussion

What is the difference between the Advanced Success Plan and Preferred Success?
The Advanced Plan builds on Preferred Success but brings substantial extensions. Hands-on Activation allows SAP experts, for the first time, to activate functionalities directly in customer systems and guide lightweight configurations. On top of that, there is AI-powered guidance through Cloud ALM, improved SLAs, and broader coverage across the full Business Suite. Worth noting is that elements from the Preferred Success Expanded Edition, which used to be an even more expensive tier,  are now included in the Advanced Plan. In effect, customers get more for less.

Is there a maximum number of consulting days or tickets in the Advanced Plan?
No. The number of services in the Advanced Plan is not capped. Customers can book as many services as they need, as often as they need them.

What are Success Packs?
Success Packs are thematic bundles of related services — for example "Clean Core", "Finance Expansion", or "Demand Planning". They serve as a navigation aid and shift the conversation from individual service tickets to business topics and processes. Customers are not required to book all the services contained in a pack.

What role does the implementation partner play in the Success Plan model?
SAP does not position the Success Plans as competition to implementation partners. Hands-on Activation needs to be coordinated with the project team and, where applicable, the partner. SAP provides expertise on best practices, standards, and architecture — implementation work can stay with the partner.

Do I need to use Cloud ALM to benefit from the Advanced Plan?
For the full value of the AI-powered guidance, Cloud ALM is a prerequisite. The other services — Activation, Adoption Guidance, Expert Advisory — can be used independently of Cloud ALM.

Who are the experts delivering the Advanced services?
The experts are experienced SAP consultants from project work and seasoned Customer Success professionals who deliver services for the Advanced and, in part, the Max Plan. This is not a pure support organisation.

Which SAP solutions have Service Maps?
Service Maps are available for S/4HANA Public, S/4HANA Private, Supply Chain/IBP, Ariba, SuccessFactors, Customer Experience (Sales & Service Cloud, Commerce Cloud, Marketing Cloud), BTP, and Business Data Cloud.

How does the Advanced Plan support Business AI specifically?
The Advanced Plan includes expert advisory on AI governance and compliance, workshops to identify AI use cases, AI Business Readiness Workshops, and the activation of Joule, Agents, and intelligent apps. It supports you along every stage of your AI adoption journey. For taking identified use cases into prototyping, the Max Plan is required.

Is there any market evidence yet?
Yes. In the first two months after launch, more than 200 customers purchased the Advanced Success Plan. Independent analyses by IDC and Forrester confirm that customers with Success Plans reach their project goals faster and with greater confidence.

entry.title: SAP Green Ledger: Q1-26 Release Updates and Highlights
entry.content:
With this blog post, I would like to introduce a regular quarterly update series highlighting the latest innovations and enhancements in SAP Green Ledger. These updates provide an overview of new features and improvements delivered during the last quarter, helping you stay up to date with ongoing developments.

Short introduction: What is SAP Green Ledger? SAP Green Ledger supports organizations in integrating carbon into financial processes. It runs as an application on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) and integrates with SAP S/4HANA Cloud–based finance systems. It enables organizations to monitor and document carbon emission impacts at transaction level and link emissions to financial objects. By using established financial structures such as accounts, company codes, and controlling objects, carbon emissions can be consistently assigned to business activities and financial categories. This supports transparent and auditable reporting, as well as data-driven operational and strategic decision-making.

With Q1 2026, SAP Green Ledger continues to evolve, supporting organizations in moving beyond emissions tracking toward managing carbon as a financial dimension. The features released in the first quarter of this year bring a range of enhancements in carbon liability management, regulatory readiness, and financial integration, along with improvements that make everyday processes more transparent and flexible. This blog post covers the following new features:

  • CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities
  • Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates
  • Linking Carbon Data to Projects
  • Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems
  • Posting on Blocked Accounts
  • Reversing Manual Journal Entries
  • Further Updates and Improvements

 

CBAM Functionality: Configuring Carbon Certificate Data and Managing Carbon Liabilities

One of the key highlights this quarter is the support for carbon certificate data and carbon liabilities. Carbon certificates and liabilities play a central role in regulatory frameworks such as the EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), where companies must cover embedded emissions with certificates that carry a financial value. Since the EU CBAM came into effect in January 2026, companies that import specific goods into the EU are now required to report embedded emissions in these imported goods and build up corresponding carbon liabilities. While the actual purchase of EU CBAM certificates will only begin in 2027, organizations already need to track and value these obligations today.

To support these new requirements, the new Configure Carbon Certificate Data app provides a centralized entry point for maintaining the data required for carbon certificate handling and valuation. You can create and maintain Emissions Trading System (ETS) prices, which represent the cost of emitting one metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2e). Since these prices fluctuate over time and directly impact liability valuation, maintaining them each calendar week helps ensure accuracy and alignment with market developments.

Maintaining Weekly EU ETS Prices (Illustrative Values Only)

In addition, you can display available company codes and access yearly CBAM factors. These factors determine the portion of embedded emissions that must be covered by CBAM certificates. By bringing ETS pricing, company context, and CBAM factors together, the app creates a consistent foundation that connects configuration with carbon data collection, liability calculation, and journal entry processing.

The flow is further supported by enhancements in the Manage Carbon Collections app. When creating a carbon collection, you can now indicate whether carbon certificates are required and maintain relevant information such as the number of certificates to surrender. This ensures that certificate requirements are captured consistently at the point of data entry. The information can then be used in later processing steps.

Carbon Certificate -relevant Carbon Collection

Building on this, the new Manage Carbon Liabilities app provides a dedicated space to view and evaluate carbon liabilities resulting from carbon collections that require certificates. You can analyze both the number of certificates to surrender as well as their corresponding monetary value based on ETS prices, reflecting the financial obligations associated with embedded emissions.

A key part of this process is fair value calculation. The system now calculates the fair value of carbon liabilities automatically for the current calendar year. This calculation is triggered whenever ETS prices are created, updated, or deleted, as well as when carbon collections requiring certificates are posted to the journal. Fair values are calculated by multiplying the number of certificates to surrender by the latest quarterly average ETS price. The calculated fair value covers the period from January 1 to the selected posting date within the year. At the same time, you can still trigger fair value calculations manually. This allows you to recalculate values when needed while keeping posting logic consistent.

Transparency is further improved through integration with journal entries as in the Manage Journal Entries app, you can review data originating from carbon collections. This includes, next to the line items for the collected GHG emissions, CBAM embedded emissions and certificate quantities now.

Journal Entries for CBAM-relevant Carbon Collection

Together, these capabilities provide greater transparency and control over how carbon liabilities are valued and reflected in financial processes. For more information, check SAP Help: Carbon Certificate Management. Looking ahead, we will continue to further develop support in this area. While the current focus is on supporting the calculation and management of carbon liabilities, future developments will address the next phase of EU CBAM. Starting from 2027, companies will be required to purchase CBAM certificates to cover their reported emissions. To support this, SAP Green Ledger will be extended with dedicated certificate management capabilities.

 

Determining Mandatory Accounts for Carbon Certificates

To support CBAM-relevant journal entry postings, account determination for carbon certificates has been enhanced with the Determine Mandatory Accounts app (formerly Manage Carbon-Only Accounts). It enables you to assign specific CBAM accounts to charts of accounts and company codes, supporting transactions such as liability recognition and fair value adjustments. This setup is mandatory for organizations subject to EU CBAM requirements. The app provides predefined CBAM account types for assets, liabilities, and operating expenses, which are typically maintained in a connected finance system and used in SAP Green Ledger. Postings to these accounts are generated automatically, ensuring alignment with underlying carbon processes.

Accounts Assignment for EU CBAM Accounts

Check out SAP Help for more information: Assign Carbon Certificate Accounts.

 

Linking Carbon Data to Projects

Carbon data can now be linked to projects and WBS (work breakdown structure) elements. A new WBS element field is available across the apps Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, Display Account Balances, and the Carbon Data Dashboard. This allows you to link carbon emissions directly to specific WBS elements. For that, project and WBS master data can now be imported from a connected finance system using our master data import capabilities and viewed in the new Display Projects app. This enhancement enables you to gain a more structured and detailed view of carbon data and makes it possible to analyze emissions at the level of individual projects and WBS elements.

Example Project in the Display Projects App

 

Supporting Multiple SAP Sustainability Footprint Management Systems

Another improvement is the increased flexibility in system integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management systems. Before, you could only connect one business system with the role SAP Sustainability Footprint Management to a SAP Green Ledger system. Now it is possible to create multiple business systems with that role. This allows you to work with more than one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system, for example when importing carbon data from different sources. At the same time, each finance business system can be associated with only one SAP Sustainability Footprint Management system connected to a SAP Green Ledger system. This clear assignment ensures consistent and well-structured data integration. The setup is handled through business system configuration, where you define the system, assign its role, and maintain integration details such as the connected finance system.

 

Posting on Blocked Accounts

Posting behaviour has been made more flexible in SAP Green Ledger. Carbon collections can now be posted on accounts even if they are currently marked as blocked in the system. This is particularly relevant in scenarios where the account status may differ between SAP Green Ledger and the connected finance system. This can occur because account data in SAP Green Ledger is not updated in real time but is instead based on data imports from the finance system. As a result, the status in SAP Green Ledger may not always reflect the most current state.

Warning Message when Posting on a Blocked Account

Against this background, postings can still be carried out even if an account is indicated as blocked in SAP Green Ledger at a given point in time. Before posting, a warning message informs you if the account is marked as blocked. To ensure accuracy, you can verify the actual status of the account directly in the connected finance business system. Overall, this adjustment allows you to continue processes without interruption while still maintaining transparency and control over account usage.

 

Reversing Manual Journal Entries

You can now reverse manual journal entries in our Manage Journal Entries app, which makes it easier to correct postings and maintain accurate carbon data records. Reversing journal entries is possible if certain conditions are met, such as having the appropriate source type and not having been reversed previously. Once reversed, the system automatically creates corresponding journal entries in which debits and credits are swapped, ensuring that the financial impact is fully offset while preserving traceability.

The reversal information, including status, who initiated the reversal and when as well as the reversed journal entries, is available in the journal entry document, allowing you to track changes and maintain compliance with accounting standards. This enhancement helps ensure data accuracy while supporting controlled correction processes within SAP Green Ledger. For more details, have a look at SAP Help: Reverse Journal Entries.

 

Further Updates and Improvements

In addition to the key capabilities described above, several additional enhancements have been introduced to improve usability and transparency:

  • A new Journal Entry Source Type field is now available in key apps such as Manage Journal Entries, Display Line Items, and Display Account Balances and provides visibility into how journal entries were created (e.g., through allocation processes, liability valuation, carbon collection postings, imports, or manual entries and reversals).
  • Updates to the allocation functionality include the renaming of the allocation type Manual Allocation to Periodic Allocation and related UI adjustments.
  • Certain allocation options have been streamlined, such as the removal of the allocation type Account Balance Allocation from the Display Account Balances app.
  • External commenting is now available in the User Guide, enabling direct feedback and collaboration, see also Blog Post.

 

Additional Information

If you want to dive deeper into SAP Green Ledger, the SAP Help Portal is a good place to start. It provides detailed information on concepts, configuration, and application usage. You can also check the What’s New section, which is updated with every release and gives a complete overview of the latest additions. To see what's coming next, the SAP Road Map Explorer offers insight into planned features and upcoming innovations. It is updated regularly and outlines how the solution will continue to evolve.

entry.title: Celebrating my 300th blog post on the SAP Community
entry.content:
I noticed that my last blog post was my 300th on the SAP Community. In those 300 posts, I have covered many topics related to user experience, clustered into a few key areas. To celebrate this milestone, I’ll reflect on how the topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP, what I learned from the community, what’s next, and end with a call to action for you.

My 300 blogs posts contain a historical account on the evolution of SAP’s user experience

Product Marketing is much easier with great products

Before going into the content, let me acknowledge that there is no product marketing without product. All these blog posts about our UX products would not have happened without the amazing development, product management, design, QA, documentation, and other colleagues that build and test these solutions. I’m grateful to the many coworkers over the years who came up with ideas to solve real customer pain points regarding SAP’s user experience. I also appreciate the many customers and partners that provided ideas for blogs posts in online forums, SAP events, and user groups.

The topics I covered mirrored the evolution of user experience at SAP

In my product marketing role, most of my posts fell into one of these broad categories:

  • Product
  • Strategy
  • Events
  • Customer and partner enablement

The topic mix shifted over time as our team expanded the products we worked on.

Product

  • Release announcements.
    Product-focused posts generally followed the cadence of our release cycles. This shifted from annual or semi-annual service packs for on-prem solutions to continuous innovation / continuous delivery pipelines for our cloud-based tools. My first five years of posts were on SAP Screen Personas. From version 1.0’s first appearance at TechEd 2012 through the release announcements of version 3 SP18, after which Rohith Deraje took over for SP19 and beyond.
    SAP Fiori elements and SAP Fiori tools were next to join our team’s portfolio. I posted some of the early announcements for those products until Katrin Polloczek, Christoph Gollmick, Ashley Tung, and Ian Quigley took ownership of the content.
    Collaborative ERP was a more recent addition to SAP UX Foundation. I posted the early announcements on our strategy and early versions until Regina Sheynblat picked this up.
    Embedded AI in SAP Fiori apps is the newest part of our portfolio, with Conrad Bernal now posting about new innovations and beta opportunities.
  • Roadmap updates – when you have a product that 1000s of customers use to run their business, they want to know what’s coming next. Towards that end, I drafted many roadmap updates over the years on the various solutions in our portfolio.
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQ) documents – one of the most interesting parts of my role is interacting with customers in various forums and hearing their questions. Often, we hear the same questions from multiple people. In those cases, it’s worth capturing them into an FAQ document.
  • Technical tips and tricks – as part of the product team, many customers reach out vie email to ask questions that are more detailed than can be covered in an FAQ document. If their inquiry is relevant to a broader audience, I put together tips they can use to get started on a project or break through a barrier they are facing and post it so others in the SAP Community can learn.
  • Customer and partner success stories – sometimes our customers want to share their success with SAP UX solutions. In those cases, I have helped them tell their story through the SAP Community.

Strategy

When I looked through my blog posts over time, they followed the same evolution of SAP’s broader UX strategy. We built SAP Screen Personas to improve the SAP GUI user experience, one screen at a time. SAP Fiori elements accomplished this at scale, for the most common business processes. Then we rolled out SAP Fiori tools to make it easier to build Fiori elements apps. Then collaborative ERP to help people team up to solve complex business problems. Most recently, we added AI to both the Fiori apps that we ship and the tools we (and you) use to build them.

Posts on UX strategy explain how all the pieces fit together so customers can plan their digital transformation, SAP S/4HANA migration, or other strategic initiative knowing where SAP is heading. Since customers are not concerned about our internal organizational structures, I blogged on solutions within the UX Foundation portfolio as well as adjacent solutions such as SAP Build, BTP, and Joule. Sometimes we include a peek behind the curtain, so people understand how SAP Fiori powers the apps in SAP S/4HANA and what to expect as we continue to add both simplicity and power to our flagship SAP Cloud ERP solution.

Events

It’s natural to blog about events to help people set the right expectations, plan their agendas, and make the most of their investment in attending the event. Typically, I like to highlight the sessions most relevant to the customers and partners that follow me on the SAP Community and LinkedIn.

For Sapphire 2026, I have a list of recommended sessions on AI and UX, along with what I’m planning with Nicole Smythe on bridging the skills gap to accelerate your AI projects.

My other posts have covered SAP TechEd, ASUG Tech Connect, ASUG chapter meetings, and, of course, our own SAP UX Innovation Day events. Next one is on June 11 in Palo Alto, if you are interested in attending.

Customer and partner enablement

I also noticed a number of blog posts on various training courses for customers, partners, and colleagues. In the early days, we created several openSAP training courses. The ones that are still relevant have moved to learning.sap.com. We also have posts on hands-on exercises from other events or tutorials from our development experts. It makes sense that people appreciate training from the team that builds and supports the products.

The SAP community is for learning, connecting, and listening

I would be remiss if I did not reflect on the SAP Community and the role it played in my personal growth at SAP. I learned so much from the questions and comments that people submitted, and through my interactions with the highly professional SAP Community team.

Much more than the number of blog posts I have written, I’m most proud of how many other colleagues I inspired to start blogging; that list continues to grow.

And thanks to my long-time friend, former colleague, and co-author, Michael Procopio, for getting me started on my blogging journey back in our days at HP Software.

The next UI will build from our Fiori experience and use AI heavily

While SAP Fiori solves many issues, we are still working to simplify how customers consume SAP and developers create apps. Needless to say, AI will play an increasingly important role in both of these dimensions. Your ongoing feedback will continue to drive our development priorities.
You will hear more about this at Sapphire and in our next SAP UX Innovation Day.

In addition to providing your feedback through the SAP Community, you are welcome to join our monthly SAP Developer Roundtable to share your thoughts on developing SAP Fiori apps using our latest tools and technologies.
If you are a public cloud customer, you can join our online user group to discuss UX and adjacent topics with your peers and SAP experts.

 

We will continue to address your concerns about usability in SAP Cloud ERP.

To count to 300, start with 1: What are you waiting for?

A much bigger milestone than blog 300 is blog #1.
What are you waiting for? There’s no time like the present!
The SAP Community needs your voice. If you want to brainstorm ideas or need help outlining your first post, please let me know. Like the rest of the SAP Community team, I’m here to help.

Thanks for reading all my stuff over the years.

entry.title: CxO Payment Approval UX in My Inbox for Payment Requisitions - China
entry.content:
Why CxO’s Experience in Payment Approvals matters in Cloud ERP

In many Cloud ERP implementations, payment approval workflows are designed to “work”—but not necessarily to delight. That distinction matters more than most teams realize, especially when the final approvers are C‑level executives.

During a recent SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition deployment in China, a critical usability gap surfaced in the payment requisition approval experience for CxOs. The issue wasn’t functionality. It was experience.

And experience, at the executive level, determines adoption, cycle time, and ultimately trust in the system.

This is exactly why the new SAP Influence idea
“CxO Payment Approval UX in My Inbox for Payment Requisitions - China”
(described in https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/372233/?section=sectionDetails ) is so critical.

The Executive Reality: Approvals Happen in Between Everything Else

For CxOs—CFOs, CEOs, COOs, and senior finance leaders—payment approvals are rarely a primary task. They happen:

  • Between meetings
  • On mobile phones or tablets
  • Under time pressure
  • With legal and governance responsibility

In this context, every extra click matters.

Yet in the standard “My Inbox for Payment Requisitions – China” app, executives are required to navigate across six separate tabs just to understand one approval:

  • General Information
  • Requested Items
  • Payment Strategy
  • Attachments
  • Approval Details
  • Administrative Data

Critical business context—purchase orders, costs, margins, and original documents—are fragmented across the interface. The result Cognitive overload at exactly the moment when clarity is essential.

Where the Current Experience Falls Short

From an executive’s perspective, several pain points quickly emerge:

  1. No Information at a Glance

There is no single screen that answers the core executive questions:

  • What am I approving?
  • Why does it cost this much?
  • Is the documentation complete?
  • Who has approved this already?

The need to jump between tabs breaks decision flow.

  1. Attachments Are Out of Sight

Invoices, contracts, and supporting documents—often legally required—sit behind a separate tab. Executives can’t even tell at a glance whether documents exist.

  1. Missing Business-Critical Fields

Key data such as:

  • Purchase Order reference
  • Material or service details
  • Unit price and total amount
  • Gross margin

are either hidden or unavailable, forcing blind approvals or unnecessary rejection loops.

  1. Approval Actions Are Easy to Miss

Approve and Reject buttons are not consistently visible, especially on mobile devices, adding friction to the simplest part of the task: making the decision.

Rethinking Payment Approval Through a CxO Lens

The proposed redesign flips the paradigm from transaction-first to decision-first.

The guiding principle is simple:

Give executives everything they need to decide—on one screen, on any device, in seconds.

A Single, Scrollable Approval Page

All relevant information—financial data, line items, attachments, and approval history—appears on one consolidated page. No tabs. No hunting.

Business Context Front and Center

Key fields like Purchase Order number, material description, unit price, total amount (tax inclusive), and gross margin are surfaced immediately, enabling informed decisions.

Inline Attachments

Original business documents are visible directly within the approval screen. Executives can verify compliance without leaving context.

Clear Workflow Visibility

Approval steps and previous approvers are displayed inline alongside the business data, reinforcing accountability and governance.

Mobile-First by Design

The layout adapts naturally to phones and tablets, allowing approvals to happen wherever executives are—without compromising clarity.

Why CxO UX Is Not Optional in Cloud ERP

Improving executive UX isn’t a “nice-to-have.” It’s strategic.

Faster Cash Flow

Approval delays directly impact supplier payments and working capital. Removing friction accelerates decision-making.

Higher Adoption

If executives dislike the system, they will bypass it. That behavior quickly cascades through the organization.

Stronger Governance

Executives are legally and fiduciary responsible for approvals. Clear, complete information protects both the individual and the organization.

Better Customer References

Satisfied executives become champions—inside the company and in the market. Frustrated ones become blockers.

A Bigger Lesson for ERP Design

This case highlights a broader truth about Cloud ERP:

The most important users are often the least frequent users.

Designing for CxOs forces teams to prioritize:

  • Clarity over completeness
  • Insight over data volume
  • Decisions over transactions

When an approval experience works for a CEO on a mobile phone, it will work for everyone else too.

Final Thoughts

Payment approval is where finance, compliance, and executive accountability intersect. If the experience fails at this point, the entire ERP transformation is at risk.

By reimagining the payment approval UX around CxO needs, organizations don’t just improve a screen—they unlock faster operations, stronger governance, and greater confidence in their Cloud ERP investment.

Because when executives say “This system works for me,” the transformation truly succeeds.

entry.title: A Look Under the Hood: SAP's Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI)
entry.content:
What is ABTI?

The Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) is the connection point between SAP's Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) and Accounting. After FIBTI receives business transaction data from a sender application, it calls ABTI to transform that data into a journal entry in Accounting.

Why was ABTI developed?

A key motivation behind ABTI is decoupling.

First, operational and non‑Finance applications (such as Payroll or Real Estate) are decoupled from Accounting‑specific logic. Through FIBTI, sender applications no longer need to deal with Accounting concerns like G/L account determination or valuation and can remain focused on their own business domain.
Second, ABTI introduces decoupling within Finance itself: business transactions received via FIBTI are handed over to Accounting independently, ensuring that Accounting-specific processing is clearly separated and centrally handled.

Previously, sender applications had to handle a lot of Accounting-specific logic themselves – including G/L account determination and currency valuation. ABTI moves all of that responsibility into Accounting, keeping sender applications focused on their own domain.

How does it work – at a high level?

ABTI's processing happens in two main phases:

  • Business transaction data is transformed into a preliminary journal entry. This logic differs depending on the transaction type, because a payroll posting looks very different from a bank fee, for example.
  • The preliminary journal entry is then processed further – G/L accounts are determined, currencies are converted, account assignments are derived, and period and budget checks are performed.

Good to know

Most users won't probably interact with ABTI directly. But knowing it exists, and what it does, can be useful when troubleshooting, configuring, or simply trying to understand how the pieces fit together. Especially because ABTI follows a posting logic that differs significantly from classic postings, it introduces new substitution concepts, and some classic BAdIs and substitutions are not processed in ABTI postings.

You can find more information on ABTI in the SAP Help Portal under Accounting Business Transaction Interface (ABTI) | SAP Help Portal.

And if you are interested in more theory regarding the financial business transaction interface (FIBTI), you can read the Posting Logic of FIBTI: How SAP Finance Transactions Are Orchestrated - SAP Community blog or go to the SAP Help Portal under Finance Business Transaction Interface (FIBTI) | SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: Application Jobs in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud: End-to-End Guide
entry.content:
Application Jobs in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud: End-to-End Guide — Catalog, Template, Authorization, Value Help and Scheduling

If you have spent any time developing in S/4HANA Public Cloud, you have probably had a request along the lines of: "can we just have this run automatically every night?" maybe a nightly cleanup, a mass update, or a periodic data push. Your first instinct might be to reach for a classic batch program, but in S/4HANA Public Cloud, that's not how it works anymore.

The answer is Application Jobs. They are the cloud successor to the old SM36/SM37 batch job world, and once you understand the moving parts, they're actually pretty clean. The problem is that most guides I've come across either uses legacy interfaces or skip over the authorization wiring entirely. If you've ever stared at a blank "Application Jobs" Fiori app wondering why your job isn't showing up, you'll know what I mean.

This post walks through the whole thing, start to finish. We'll build a job that mass-sets the Delivery Completed indicator on Purchase Order items, using the released SAP RAP Business Object I_PurchaseOrderTP and EML MODIFY ENTITIES statements. I chose this scenario because it's a genuine business requirement that procurement teams deal with regularly, and it gives us a proper reason to use EML rather than just reading data.

Here's everything we'll cover:

  1. Implement business logic for the application job
  2. CDS views for value helps
  3. Application Job Catalog Entry
  4. Application Job Template
  5. Identity and Access Management (IAM)
    • 5.1 IAM App
    • 5.2 Business Catalog
    • 5.3 Business Role and user assignment
  6. Scheduling and monitoring
  7. Accessing Application Logs

Old interfaces vs IF_APJ_RT_RUN

Older blog posts and some documentation reference two legacy interfaces: IF_APJ_DT_EXEC_OBJECT (design-time) and IF_APJ_RT_EXEC_OBJECT (runtime). IF_APJ_RT_RUN replaces both with a single interface and one method: EXECUTE. Parameters are declared as public class attributes.

This guide uses IF_APJ_RT_RUN throughout.

Step 1: Implement Business Logic for the Application Job

To implement the business logic, create an ABAP class that implements IF_APJ_RT_RUN. Any public attributes defined in the class will automatically become the selection parameters for the Application Job.

Right-click your package in ADT → New → ABAP Class.

CLASS zcl_job_po_delivery_complete DEFINITION

  PUBLIC

  FINAL

  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    INTERFACES if_apj_rt_run.

    INTERFACES if_apj_dt_defaults. "-- optional: provides default values for the

    "-- selection parameters

    DATA company_code  TYPE RANGE OF bukrs.

    DATA purch_org     TYPE RANGE OF ekorg.

    DATA document_date TYPE RANGE OF bedat.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_job_po_delivery_complete IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_apj_dt_defaults~fill_attribute_defaults.

    "-- Set default values for new templates.

    "-- The framework calls this when a new template is created for this class.

    "-- Leave empty if you don't need pre-filled defaults.

    company_code = VALUE #( ( sign = 'I' option = 'EQ' low = '001' ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_apj_rt_run~execute.

    "--- The attributes company_code, purch_org, document_date are automatically

    "    filled by the framework before this method is called. Use them directly.

    "--- 1. Find eligible PO items -----------------------------------------------

    SELECT

      item~purchaseorder,

      item~purchaseorderitem

    FROM i_purchaseorderitemtp_2 AS item

    INNER JOIN i_purchaseordertp_2 AS po

      ON po~purchaseorder = item~purchaseorder

    WHERE po~companycode            IN @company_code

      AND po~purchasingorganization IN @purch_org

      AND po~purchaseorderdate      IN @document_date

      AND item~iscompletelydelivered = @ABAP_false

    INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_po_items).

    "--- 2. Set up the Application Log -------------------------------------------

    TRY.

      DATA(lo_log) = cl_bali_log=>create_with_header(

        cl_bali_header_setter=>create(

          object    = 'ZAPJ_PO'

          subobject = 'DELIV_COMPL' ) ).

    CATCH cx_bali_runtime.

      "handle exception

    ENDTRY.

    IF lt_po_items IS INITIAL.

      TRY.

        lo_log->add_item( item = cl_bali_message_setter=>create_from_sy( ) ).

        cl_bali_log_db=>get_instance( )->save_log(

          log                        = lo_log

          assign_to_current_appl_job = abap_true ).

      CATCH cx_bali_runtime ##NO_HANDLER.

      ENDTRY.

      COMMIT WORK.

      RETURN.

    ENDIF.

    "--- 3. Build EML MODIFY input -----------------------------------------------

    "    The %key for a child entity must include both the root key (PurchaseOrder)

    "    and the item key (PurchaseOrderItem). The %control flag tells the

    "    framework which fields we actually intend to change.

    DATA lt_update_input TYPE TABLE FOR UPDATE i_purchaseordertp_2\\purchaseorderitem.

    LOOP AT lt_po_items INTO DATA(ls_item).

      APPEND VALUE #(

        %key-purchaseorder             = ls_item-purchaseorder

        %key-purchaseorderitem         = ls_item-purchaseorderitem

        iscompletelydelivered          = abap_true

        %control-iscompletelydelivered = if_abap_behv=>mk-on

      ) TO lt_update_input.

    ENDLOOP.

    "--- 4. EML MODIFY -----------------------------------------------------------

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_purchaseordertp_2

      ENTITY purchaseorderitem

      UPDATE FIELDS ( iscompletelydelivered )

      WITH lt_update_input

      FAILED   DATA(lt_failed)

      REPORTED DATA(lt_reported).

    "--- 5. Commit — but only if something actually succeeded --------------------

    DATA(lv_failed_count) = lines( lt_failed-purchaseorderitem ).

    DATA(lv_total_count)  = lines( lt_po_items ).

    IF lv_failed_count < lv_total_count.

      COMMIT ENTITIES

        RESPONSE OF i_purchaseordertp_2

        FAILED   DATA(lt_commit_failed)

        REPORTED DATA(lt_commit_reported).

    ENDIF.

    "--- 6. Log summary ----------------------------------------------------------

    TRY.

      lo_log->add_item(

        cl_bali_free_text_setter=>create(

          severity = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_status

          text     = |PO Delivery Completion Job finished: | &

                     |{ lv_total_count - lv_failed_count } item(s) updated, | &

                     |{ lv_failed_count } item(s) failed.| ) ).

      "--- 7. Log individual failures --------------------------------------------

      LOOP AT lt_failed-purchaseorderitem INTO DATA(ls_fail).

        lo_log->add_item(

          cl_bali_free_text_setter=>create(

            severity = if_bali_constants=>c_severity_error

            text     = |Failed: PO { ls_fail-%key-purchaseorder } | &

                       |Item { ls_fail-%key-purchaseorderitem }| ) ).

      ENDLOOP.

      "--- 8. Surface RAP BO validation messages (e.g. "document locked") --------

      LOOP AT lt_reported-purchaseorderitem INTO DATA(ls_rep).

        lo_log->add_item(

          cl_bali_exception_setter=>create( exception = CAST #( ls_rep-%msg ) ) ).

      ENDLOOP.

      "--- 9. Save log — linked to the current application job ------------------

      cl_bali_log_db=>get_instance( )->save_log(

        log                        = lo_log

        assign_to_current_appl_job = abap_true ).

    CATCH cx_bali_runtime.

      "handle exception

    ENDTRY.

    COMMIT WORK.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Step 2: Value Help CDS Views

When a user fills in the job parameters before scheduling, we want proper F4 dialogs rather than free-text fields. The Application Job Catalog Entry supports assigning a CDS view as the value help for each parameter.

We'll wrap two released SAP views,  I_CompanyCode and I_PurchasingOrganization, in thin custom CDS views, Z_I_CompanyCodeVH and Z_I_PURCHORG_VH.

Make sure to include @ObjectModel.dataCategory: #VALUE_HELP in both views. Without it, the catalog entry editor will not allow you to select them as value helps.

Company Code value help

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Company Code'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@ObjectModel.dataCategory: #VALUE_HELP

@search.searchable: true

define view entity Z_I_CompanyCodeVH as select from I_CompanyCode

{

  @search.defaultSearchElement: true

  key CompanyCode,

  @search.defaultSearchElement: true

  CompanyCodeName

}

Purchasing Organization value help

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Purchasing Organization'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@ObjectModel.dataCategory: #VALUE_HELP

@search.searchable: true

define view entity Z_I_PURCHORG_VH as select from I_PurchasingOrganization

{

  @search.defaultSearchElement: true

  key PurchasingOrganization,

  @search.defaultSearchElement: true

  PurchasingOrganizationName,

  @search.defaultSearchElement: true

  CompanyCode

}

Step 3: Application Job Catalog Entry

The Catalog Entry tells the framework which class to run, defines how selection parameters are displayed, and assigns value helps. Since your class implements IF_APJ_RT_RUN, when you point the Catalog Entry at your class the parameter table is automatically populated from the class's public attributes. You don't add parameters manually row by row.

Right-click your package → New → Other ABAP Repository Object → search for Application Job Catalog Entry → Next.

There are quite a few options for defining the properties of your parameters; you can mark one as mandatory, render it as a checkbox, radio button, and so on. The Sections and Groups tabs let you organise how parameters appear on the selection screen. None of this was possible with the old IF_APJ_DT_EXEC_OBJECT / IF_APJ_RT_EXEC_OBJECT interfaces.

Activate the catalog entry.

Step 4: Application Job Template

The Template sits on top of the Catalog Entry and is what business users actually pick when they schedule a job.

Right-click your package → New → Other ABAP Repository Object → Application Job Template.

When the template is created, the framework instantiates the class and calls IF_APJ_DT_DEFAULTS~FILL_ATTRIBUTE_DEFAULTS (if implemented) to pre-populate default values. You can see in the screenshot that the default Company Code is already filled in.

Activate the template.

Step 5: Identity and Access Management (IAM)

Step 5.1: IAM App

Even if your class, catalog entry, and template are all perfectly correct, a business user won't be able to schedule the job until it's been wired up through the IAM framework. Until the IAM objects exist, the Job Template simply won't appear in the Application Jobs Fiori app.

The good news: when you create a job catalog entry and a job template, the IAM App is generated automatically. No manual steps are needed here.

Step 5.2: Business Catalog

The Business Catalog bundles one or more IAM Apps into a group that can be assigned to a Business Role. You can either create a new Business Catalog or assign the IAM App to an existing one; both options are available directly from the IAM App itself.

For this blog, I'm creating a new Business Catalog.

Once you create the assignment, you'll see an assignment object linking your IAM App to the new Business Catalog.

Don't forget to publish the catalog - click the Publish Locally button. Once done, the status changes to "Published".

Step 5.3: Business Role and User Assignment

You need to add the business catalog to a business role. You can either create a new one or add it to an existing role. For this blog, let's create a new business role.

We now leave ADT and switch to the Fiori Launchpad. Open the Maintain Business Roles app and click New.

In the Assigned Business Catalogs tab, add catalog Z_DEMO_BC_ADMIN.

Save the role.

Assign the role to a user

Open Maintain Business Users, search for your user, and open the detail page. Click Edit, go to Assigned Business Roles, add your business role, and save.

You may need to restart the Fiori launchpad for the role to take effect.

Step 6: Schedule and Monitor

Open the Application Jobs app from the Fiori Launchpad and click Create. In the Job Template field, select your job template. Give the job a descriptive name, something like Set Delivery Completed – Company Code 1010 – Q1 2026.

Click Step 2. In the Scheduling Options section, either run immediately or set up a recurrence pattern. For this demo, check Start Immediately.

Click Step 3 and fill in the parameters. This is where the value help work pays off: clicking the F4 icon on Company Code opens a searchable list instead of a blank text field. The default value we set in the class is pre-filled as well.

Click Schedule.

Checking the results

If the job finishes without errors, the status shows as "Finished", otherwise "Failed". You can also copy a job, cancel a running one, or restart a job chain directly from the app.

Step 7: Accessing Application Logs

  • Application Jobs Fiori app - once the job finishes, click the entry to open the detail view, then click the Log Status to view the application log messages.

  • Display Application Logs app / SLG1 - search across jobs by log object, sub-object, user, date, or severity.

What we built — a quick recap

Object

Type

Name

Job Class

ABAP Class

ZCL_JOB_PO_DELIVERY_COMPLETE

Value Help - Company Code

CDS View Entity

Z_I_CompanyCodeVH

Value Help - Purchasing Org

CDS View Entity

Z_I_PURCHORG_VH

Catalog Entry

Appl. Job Catalog Entry

Z_DEMO_PO_JOB_CATALOG_ENTRY

Template

Appl. Job Template

Z_DEMO_PO_JOB_TEMPLATE

IAM App

IAM App (BG)

Z_DEMO_PO_JOB_CATALOG_ENTRY_SAJC

Business Catalog

Business Catalog

Z_DEMO_BC_ADMIN

Business Role

Business Role

Z_DEMO_ADMIN

Hope this saves you some of the time I spent piecing this together.

Further Reading

The following resources complement this guide, either as the official reference, or as community posts covering adjacent patterns and earlier interface versions.

SAP Official Documentation

  • Application Jobs — SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - the official SAP Help Portal reference covering the framework architecture, interfaces, and configuration options.
  • Application Logs

Related SAP Community Posts

  • How to Implement Application Jobs which Automatically Update Sales Orders - WesleyZhang, July 2024. A comprehensive end-to-end walkthrough covering catalog entry, template, IAM, and Business Role setup. Uses the older IF_APJ_DT_EXEC_OBJECT / IF_APJ_RT_EXEC_OBJECT interface pattern predating the 2502 release.
  • How to Do Background Scheduling with Clean Core, Including Fiori Apps - SAP, April 2025. SAP's introduction of IF_APJ_RT_RUN - the single unified interface that replaces the older dual-interface pattern, available from Public Cloud release 2502 onwards.
  • F4 Help for Application Job in Public Cloud (2502 Release) - SAP, February 2025. Covers the value help improvements introduced in the 2502 release, directly relevant to the CDS value help approach used in this guide.
  • How to Schedule Application Jobs from a RAP-Based Business Object - SAP, December 2022. If you want to go further and trigger Application Jobs programmatically from within a RAP Business Object rather than via the Fiori scheduling UI, this post covers the API-based approach.

entry.title: 切换到 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition中全新的业务伙伴主数据批量处理的应用
entry.content:
前言

你是否仍在 SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 中使用已被弃用的业务伙伴（Business Partner，简称 BP）主数据批量导入和批量维护应用？

现在正是时候切换到新的继任应用（Successor Apps），以便在旧应用被正式移除之前完成系统准备。本篇文章将为你介绍：

  • 为什么需要进行切换
  • 新应用带来的主要改进
  • 切换所需的具体步骤和注意事项

⚠️ 重要提醒
在旧应用被彻底下线之前，你必须完成本文描述的切换操作。

当前计划：旧应用将在 SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2608 版本中移除。

为什么要切换到新的批处理应用？

新的业务伙伴主数据批处理应用在多个方面得到了显著增强：

1. 更好的易用性（Usability）

  • 启动批量变更流程时，提供了全新的对话框
  • 可以更加灵活地定义和调整选择条件
  • 选择条件可在页面顶部直接查看和进一步细化
  • 不再需要单独定义变更“范围（Scope）”

➡️ 整体操作更加直观、符合业务用户的使用习惯。

 

 

2. 批量变更与批量导入更加集中

  • 现在可以在同一个起始对话框中：
    • 直接基于文件执行批量变更
    • 或进行批量数据导入
  • “管理批量维护（Manage Mass Maintenance）” 应用成为统一入口
  • 不再需要单独的 “Start Mass Processing” 磁贴

3. 更丰富的流程属性

  • 新版本支持更多流程属性，例如：
    • 到期日期（Due Date）
    • 其他流程相关信息

4. 与 SAP MDG 的更深度集成

  • 对于已购买 SAP Master Data Governance（MDG） 许可证的客户：
    • 可在 “Manage Business Partner – Central Governance” 中查看单个业务伙伴
    • 实现业务伙伴的统一视图
  • 可通过后文提到的 Additional Scope Items 启用此能力

5. 最新的 SAP Fiori 用户体验

  • 使用最新的 SAP Fiori 设计语言
  • 支持通用 Fiori 功能，例如：
    • 与其他用户协作
    • 更一致的交互体验

我需要做什么？（切换步骤概览）

完成切换总共需要 三个基础步骤：

步骤一：完成或清理已有批量流程

  • 请先：
    • 完成所有仍在进行中的批量维护流程
    • 或删除不再需要的流程

📌 原因说明
使用旧版 “Manage Mass Maintenance” 创建的流程，在新版本应用中将不可见。

步骤二：激活新功能并调整业务目录

1️⃣ 激活功能开关

激活功能：

Successor MDG apps for business partners
(MDC_BP_CLOUD_READY)

⚠️ 注意：该功能激活后不可回退，请先在测试环境验证相关业务流程。

请打开系统配置里的Activate Features来激活此功能：

 

2️⃣ 移除已弃用的业务目录

从业务角色中移除以下目录（Deprecated）：

  • Mass Load - Business Partner Master
    SAP_CMD_BC_BP_MASSLOAD_PC
  • Mass Maintenance - Business Partner Master
    SAP_CMD_BC_BP_MASSMAINT_PC

3️⃣ 分配新的继任业务目录

添加新的业务目录：

  • Master Data Governance - Mass Maintenance for Business Partners
    SAP_CMD_BC_BPCS_MAINT_PC

这将使你的业务角色与最新版模板保持一致：

  • Master Data Specialist - Business Partner Data
    SAP_BR_BUPA_MASTER_SPECIALIST

针对2️⃣3️⃣两步的操作，你可以在维护业务角色中，找到你做批量维护的业务角色，手动删除废弃的业务目录，和添加新的业务目录：

 或者

你也可以在应用“在升级后管理业务角色更改”中，打开“不赞成使用的业务目录”页签，选择被废弃的业务目录

 然后对受影响的业务角色直接做更改。这样操作会直接把被废弃的业务目录替换成后继新的业务目录：

 

步骤三：通过导出完成技术设置（关键步骤）

这是一个强制性步骤，用于最终启用所有新应用。

操作步骤：

  1. 启动应用
    Export Master Data – Business Partners
    （Fiori ID：F6551A）

  2. 定义一个最小范围的导出，例如：

    • 表：BUT000
    • 至少选择 一个业务伙伴

  3. 执行导出

    • 不需要下载生成的文件
    • 只要导出成功完成即可

✅ 完成后，即可正式使用新应用：

  • Manage Mass Maintenance – BP（Fiori ID：F7974）
  • 以及新业务目录中包含的所有继任应用

其他扩展范围（Additional Scope Items）

如果你拥有 SAP Master Data Governance 的额外许可证，还可以启用以下 Scope Item：

  • Central Governance for Business Partner (1N7)

这将为业务伙伴主数据治理提供更多高级能力。

总结

通过切换到新的业务伙伴主数据批处理应用，你将获得：

  • 更现代化的 SAP Fiori 体验
  • 更灵活、更直观的批量处理方式
  • 与 SAP MDG 更深度的集成
  • 为未来版本升级（2608 及以后）提前做好准备

👉 建议尽早完成切换，并在生产系统启用前充分测试。
